fact: January 16 is exactly 16 days into the new year, making it the only date where the day number matches how many days have passed!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for ice fishing because lakes and ponds often have their thickest, strongest ice layers right around now.
Capricorn is still the ruling zodiac sign until January 19, so people born on this date get the stubborn, mountain-climbing goat as their astrological symbol.
January 16 hits right in the middle of the coldest stretch in many northern places, when even your breath can freeze instantly outside.
Penguins in Antarctica are having their summer right now, with some chicks typically taking their very first ocean swims around this time.
Tree sap often freezes solid inside maple tree trunks during this time of year in northern climates, turning into nature's popsicles until spring.
The sun sets very early in northern cities - kids in Alaska might not see daylight after 3 PM on January 16!
Hibernating bears in North America are usually in their deepest sleep phase right now, and mama bears might be giving birth to tiny cubs no bigger than hamsters.
January 16 falls exactly 4 weeks and 4 days after the winter solstice, meaning northern regions have typically gained back about 30 minutes of daylight already.
Wolves often howl more on cold January nights because sound travels farther in freezing air - their calls can reach other packs 6 miles away.
In the Southern Hemisphere, animals like desert foxes are typically shedding their thick winter coats right now because it's blazing summer down there.
The moon often looks extra huge and orange when it rises on cold January nights because you're looking through more frozen water crystals in the air.
